title: Modality
description: The kind of input or output data a model is built to handle, such as text, images, audio, or video.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A modality is a data type the model consumes or produces. Unimodal models specialize in one channel; multimodal models align two or more, such as image patches with text tokens. Modality sits above representation: it names what is encoded, not the internal vector format.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Modality tells you which encoders, tokenizers, and fusion modules to expect before you open a family page. It also separates "handles images" from "is a diffusion architecture" or "is a foundation model."

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A speech-to-text system pairs an audio waveform modality with a text output modality. A text-only LLM has a single text modality end to end even when users paste images in a chat UI—the product may be multimodal while the core model is not.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Modality appears in model cards, dataset documentation, and multimodal family overviews. The model glossary page links here when describing what a checkpoint accepts.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Modality is not architecture: a transformer can be text-only or multimodal depending on inputs. Modality is also not representation—a modality is the raw channel, while representation is the learned internal form after encoding.

openingSummary: Modality names the data channel—text tokens, pixels, or spectrograms—not the architecture or trained checkpoint powering a model.
